title: Compulsory education, duration (years)
definition: Duration of compulsory education is the number of years that children are legally obliged to attend school.

source.license: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International unless specifically labeled otherwise by World Bank metadata
source.sourceOrganization: Data API, UN Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO), uri: https://databrowser.uis.unesco.org/resources, note: The data are obtained through the UIS API.  Detailed documentation is available at: https://api.uis.unesco.org/api/public/documentation/, publisher: UNESCO Institute for Statistics (UIS), date accessed: 2026-03-19, date published: 2026-02

editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include Venezuela, RB, Guatemala, El Salvador, while the lightest include New Caledonia, Bangladesh, Madagascar; that contrast is the fastest way to read the compulsory education duration pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: Compulsory education duration adds a policy-shaped schooling puzzle that is not simply about income.

bestProbeCountries.reason: High anchor: this country sits near the darkest end of the map.
Low anchor: this country clarifies the lightest end of the scale.
Middle check: this country helps separate a true pattern from one-off outliers.

commonConfusions.reason: Rural population overlaps with part of the visible pattern, but the full country spread does not match.
Urban population overlaps with part of the visible pattern, but the full country spread does not match.
Urban basic drinking water overlaps with part of the visible pattern, but the full country spread does not match.

editorial.difficultyReason: Expert difficulty: the map can be mistaken for adjacent indicators unless players probe a high, low, and middle country.
regionalSignals: Education indicators can be shaped by enrollment systems and data coverage.
Compare youth-heavy countries with higher-income service economies.

reviewNotes: Batch 3 promotion: broad source coverage, fresh policy data, and a readable unit make this a fair expert Daily.
Keep answer choices away from enrollment-rate maps so the clue reads as education policy, not school attendance.
